coredata.dc:title: Sulfur-doped carbon dots@polydopamine-functionalized magnetic silver nanocubes for dual-modality detection of norovirus
coredata.dc:creator: Achadu, Ojodomo J.
Abe, Fuyuki
Hossain, Farzana
Nasrin, Fahmida
Yamazaki, Masahito
Suzuki, Tetsuro
Park, Enoch Y.
coredata.dc:description: Synergistic dual-mode optical platforms are up-and-coming detection tools in the diagnosis and management of infectious diseases. Here, novel dual-modality fluorescence (FL) and surface-enhanced Raman scattering (SERS) techniques have been integrated into a single probe for the rapid and ultrasensitive detection of norovirus (NoV). The developed FL-SER-based biosensor relies on the dual-signal enhancements of newly synthesized sulfur-doped agar-derived carbon dots (S-agCDs). The antigen-antibody immunoreaction results in forming a core-satellite immunocomplex between anti-NoV antibody-conjugated S-agCDs and polydopamine-functionalized magnetic silver nanocubes [poly (dop)-MNPs-Ag NCs]. By deploying an immunomagnetic enrichment protocol and performing the SERS modality on a single-layer graphene substrate, norovirus-like particles (NoV-LPs) were detected across a wide range of 1 fg mL−1 – 10 ng mL−1 with an excellent limit of detection of 0.1 fg mL−1. The combined advantage of the dual-signaling properties of the biosensor was demonstrated using FL confocal imaging for “hotspots” tracking prior to SERS detection of clinical NoV in fecal specimen down to ⁓10 RNA copies mL−1. The proposed dual-modality biosensor's performance increases the prospect of a rapid and low-cost sensitive NoV detection and surveillance option for public health.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon-dots
Magnetoplasmonic-Ag-nanocubes
Dual-modality
Fluorescence-SERS
Immunocomplex
Norovirus

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis and characterization of S-agCDs and poly(dop)-MNPs-Ag NCs
Working principle and design of the dual-signaling biosensor (CSSFEI)
Specific and reproducible detection of NoV by CSSFEI dual-modality biosensor

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: OJA gratefully thanks the Japan Society for the Promotion of Science ( JSPS ) for a Postdoctoral Fellowship for Research in Japan (Standard) (Grant No. 19F19348). The Japan Agency for Medical Research and Development partly supported this work (20hm0102080h0001).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization of S-agCDs. XPS survey scan (a), high resolution spectra of C1s (b) and S2p (c), TEM image (d), the corresponding size distribution histogram (e), HR-TEM (f), XRD (g), Raman spectra (h), UV–vis/FL spectra (inset: FL under visible or UV-365 nm lights) (i), and excitation-wavelength-dependent emissions (j).

FL modality detection of NoV-LPs in a PBS medium (a), or 10% human serum (b), corresponding calibration plots (c). FL confocal images of the CSSFEI, bright field (d), at 1 pg mL−1 (e) and 1 ng mL−1 of NoV-LPs (f).

SERS mapping (50 × 50) of the CSSFEI immunocomplex on SLG substrates containing NoV-LPs of 1 fg mL−1 (a), 1 pg mL−1 (b), and 1 ng mL−1 (c). SERS modality detection of NoV-LPs in PBS (d) or 10% human serum (e), and corresponding calibration plots (f).

FL-SERS dual-modality detection of the clinical fecal specimen of NoV. FL modality (a), corresponding calibration plot (b), SERS modality (c), and calibration plot (d). The error bars indicate the standard deviations of triplicate measurements.

Selectivity results for the FL (a) and SERS (b) modalities screening of NoV-LPs in the presence of other viruses. (c) optical image of an SLG substrate showing randomly mapped spots (100), (d) the corresponding SERS signals, and calculated RSD of mapped spots showing spot-to-spot reproducibility.
Synthesis and Ab conjugation of S-agCDs (a), poly (dop)-MNPs-Ag NCs (b), and the stepwise dual-modality NoV detection (c).

head.ce:title: Sulfur-doped carbon dots@polydopamine-functionalized magnetic silver nanocubes for dual-modality detection of norovirus

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Research Institute of Green Science and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Synergistic dual-mode optical platforms are up-and-coming detection tools in the diagnosis and management of infectious diseases. Here, novel dual-modality fluorescence (FL) and surface-enhanced Raman scattering (SERS) techniques have been integrated into a single probe for the rapid and ultrasensitive detection of norovirus (NoV). The developed FL-SER-based biosensor relies on the dual-signal enhancements of newly synthesized sulfur-doped agar-derived carbon dots (S-agCDs). The antigen-antibody immunoreaction results in forming a core-satellite immunocomplex between anti-NoV antibody-conjugated S-agCDs and polydopamine-functionalized magnetic silver nanocubes [poly (dop)-MNPs-Ag NCs]. By deploying an immunomagnetic enrichment protocol and performing the SERS modality on a single-layer graphene substrate, norovirus-like particles (NoV-LPs) were detected across a wide range of 1 fg mL−1 – 10 ng mL−1 with an excellent limit of detection of 0.1 fg mL−1. The combined advantage of the dual-signaling properties of the biosensor was demonstrated using FL confocal imaging for “hotspots” tracking prior to SERS detection of clinical NoV in fecal specimen down to ⁓10 RNA copies mL−1. The proposed dual-modality biosensor's performance increases the prospect of a rapid and low-cost sensitive NoV detection and surveillance option for public health.

ce:section.ce:para: A norovirus (NoV) infection activates a powerful immune response that promptly eliminates the virus. However, an infection with one NoV strain provides no resistance to another, resulting in frequent NoV infections during peak seasons (Chang et al., 2018). This requires stringent and speedy diagnostic tests, which are challenging to attain using reverse transcription-polymerase chain reaction (RT-qPCR) or enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) techniques due to multi-step viral RNA extraction and lengthy protocols (Ashiba et al., 2017; Liu and Moore, 2020; Vinjé, 2015). Hence, rapid and ultrasensitive diagnostics are still exigently needed to combat the scourge of NoV infection.

Fluorescence (FL) and surface-enhanced Raman scattering (SERS) spectroscopy are two complementary optical modalities that have been used as extraordinarily powerful analytical tools for biosensing and bio-imaging applications (Achadu et al., 2020a; Achadu and Nyokong, 2016, 2017; Achadu and Revaprasadu, 2019; Chen et al., 2020; Fu et al., 2016; Zhang and Du, 2016), with the potential for outstanding performances when combined. Meanwhile, the ultrasensitive and reliable bioassays of pathogens in clinical specimens are challenging, particularly with single modality techniques, due to the substantial matrix effects on the quality of signals (Achadu et al., 2020b; Neng et al., 2013; Sun et al., 2017; Takemura et al., 2017). For this reason, combining one detection approach with another in a dual-modality strategy may likely eliminate the inherent limitations of both techniques while providing enormous benefits (Niu et al., 2014; Zong et al., 2013; Zou et al., 2015). In this context, dual-modality approaches have been credited with boosting bioassays sensitivities across wide linear ranges with multiplexed detection capacity (Alvarez-Puebla et al., 2018; Chuong et al., 2017).

Nanosized carbon dots (CDs) with exceptional and multifunctional properties are particularly appealing for biological or chemical sensors design (Chand and Neethirajan, 2017; Loo et al., 2016; Purbia and Paria, 2016; Liu et al., 2018). They exhibit remarkable optical enhancements phenomena when coupled with plasmonic nanostructures (Khlebtsov et al., 2020; Ling et al., 2015; Song et al., 2020; Yao et al., 2020), thereby extending their applicability into analytical detection systems. This research has newly synthesized sulfur-doped agar-derived CDs (S-agCDs) as a single optical probe capable of generating both FL and Raman signals to develop a robust bimodal platform for virus detection. Our innovative synthesis strategy integrates two different materials of potato dextrose agar (PDA) and 4-mercaptobenzoic acid (4-MBA) as precursors into a single state-of-the-art nano-entity of S-agCDs via a one-pot hydrothermal process. The thiolated 4-MBA, a classical Raman reporter and the sulfur source, confers extra emissive states onto the S-agCDs for superior FL properties (Achadu and Nyokong, 2016; Achadu et al., 2016; Achadu and Revaprasadu, 2018; Gao et al., 2017; Wang et al., 2017), as well as providing distinct Raman signatures for a catalyzed SERS activity (Chen et al., 2017; Pham et al., 2019).

Magnetoplasmonic nanostructures, on the other hand, have extraordinary properties due to their synergistic plasmonic and magnetic functions (Lee et al., 2018; Neng et al., 2013; Zou et al., 2015). Silver nanocubes (Ag NCs) possess uniquely strong localized surface plasmon resonance (LSPR) that can interface with molecules or nanoprobes for optical enhancements (Rycenga et al., 2011; Zhou et al., 2016). Magnetic nanoparticles (MNPs) are used to rapidly separate and enrich target analytes from complex samples, thereby considerably improving bioassays sensitivities and eliminating matrix interferences (Lee et al., 2018; Li et al., 2018; Neng et al., 2013; Takemura et al., 2017). Thus, we strategically prepared novel magnetoplasmonic nanostructures by combining Ag NCs and MNPs using polydopamine [poly (dop)] as a stabilizing agent for the facile conjugation of biotargeting agents for the target virus capture.

Herein, dual-signaling S-agCDs are paired with poly (dop)-functionalized MNPs-coated Ag NCs [poly (dop)-MNPs-Ag NCs] to construct a core-satellite SERS-FL-encoded immunocomplex (CSSFEI) platform as an ultrasensitive dual-modality biosensor for NoV. With the aid of antibody (Ab) specific to NoV, the target NoV-LPs/NoV were initially captured by Ab-conjugated-poly (dop)-MNPs-Ag NCs to form immunocomplexes of NoV-Ab-poly (dop)-MNPs-Ag NCs. These immunocomplexes were enriched and rapidly separated by an external magnet, thanks to the MNPs' functionality. The purified immunocomplexes were further redispersed in fresh buffer solutions, followed by immunoreactions with Ab-conjugated-S-agCDs (Ab-S-agCDs) to form the CSSFEI, as shown in Scheme 1 . Following an optimized sensing protocol, it was found that poly (dop)-MNPs-Ag NCs simultaneously activated the dual signals of the surrounding S-agCDs, where the FL and SERS modes are intensified based on the concentration of the target NoV.

Furthermore, we demonstrate the proof-of-concept of the proposed dual-modality system and the unique advantage of its combined applications using FL confocal imaging of the CSSFEI on a single-layer graphene (SLG) substrate. The FL confocal imaging was used to track the generated “hotspots” of the CSSFEI prior to the SERS mapping for NoV detection. With this strategy, we successfully detected ⁓10 RNA copies mL−1 of NoV in human fecal specimens to demonstrate the practical advantage of using confocal imaging to boost the performance of our biosensor for detecting remarkable levels of NoV in real-life samples. Finally, a comparative performance evaluation shows that the proposed biosensor outperforms tested commercially available kits, thereby raising the prospects of using this system for the clinical diagnosis of NoV infectious disease.

In the Supplementary data, the following are provided in detail: Biological Materials, Clinical Samples and their RT-qPCR Quantifications (S1), Chemicals (S2), Characterization and Bioassays Equipment (S3 and S4), Materials synthesis [S-agCDs, Poly (dop)-MNPs-Ag NCs, respective anti-NoV antibody Conjugations] and Preparation of SLG-coated Substrate (S5), Dual-modality detection of NoV-LPs and Clinical NoV (S6).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of S-agCDs and poly(dop)-MNPs-Ag NCs
ce:section.ce:para: In this work, PDA (agar) was used as the principal precursor (4-MBA used as the sulfur source) to prepare highly aqueous dispersible and fluorescent S-agCDs via a simple one-pot hydrothermal route (Scheme 1a). The characterization of S-agCDs was accomplished using spectroscopic and electron microscopy techniques. First, X-ray photoemission spectroscopy (XPS) was used to clarify the chemically distinct atoms and surface states of S-agCDs. The XPS survey spectrum, aligned with the adventitious C1s binding energy of 285 eV as a reference (Greczynski and Hultman, 2020), shows prominent atomic peaks of S2p, C1s, N1s, and O1s at 165.5, 284.9, 400.2, and 532.6 eV, respectively (Fig. 1 a). The high-resolution XPS results confirmed the predominance of inter-atomic bonds including C, N, O, and S to reveal the chemical states as well as S-doping of the S-agCDs (Fig. 1b and c; Figs. S1a–c in the Supplementary data S7). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) revealed monodispersed and quasi-spherically structured S-agCDs nanoparticles (Fig. 1d), with a size distribution of 1–7 nm and a mean size of ⁓3.95 ± 0.5 nm (Fig. 1e). S-agCDs sizes >10 nm in the TEM image may be due to aggregation. A match-up HR-TEM micrograph (Fig. 1f) shows clearly separated nanoparticles of S-agCDs with no discernible lattice fringes popular in graphitic carbon-based nanoparticles, thus suggesting that the prepared S-agCDs are not graphitic in nature (Hou et al., 2015; Wei et al., 2014).

This is further complemented by the XRD pattern of the nanosized dots, which revealed a peak at 2θ = 23.60 with an interlayer spacing, d = 0.38 nm (Fig. 1g), showing the non-graphitic nature of the S-agCDs as the obtained d value is higher than that of typical graphitic materials (d = 0.33 nm) (Demchenko and Dekaliuk, 2016). The Raman spectra of S-agCDs clearly show strong absorptions at ⁓1342 cm−1 and ~1572 cm−1, respectively (Fig. 1h), which are broadened most likely due to 4-MBA doping and/or the amorphous carbon networks of the S-agCDs. The optical properties clearly show characteristic UV–vis absorption at 375 nm, with a maximum FL intensity at 465 nm when excited at 360 nm (Fig. 1i). An excitation wavelength-dependent FL behavior in the S-agCDs (Fig. 1j), is similar to other CDs (Liu et al., 2017). Further characterization results and discussion of S-agCDs are provided in Figs. S1a–e in the Supplementary data S7.

Scheme 1b illustrates poly (dop)-MNPs-Ag NCs synthesis and their Ab conjugation. Characterization and discussion detail of poly (dop)-MNPs-Ag NCs are provided in Figs. S2–S6 in the Supplementary data S8. Here, plasmonic Ag NCs were functionalized with MNPs to allow for magnetic enrichment and purification of the virus analyte from the complex matrix (Lee et al., 2018; Zhang et al., 2019; Zou et al., 2015), followed by poly (dop)-coating for aqueous solubility and stability of the MNPs-Ag NCs conjugate. Poly (dop) is also used as a capping agent and/or linker to facilitate the facile conjugation of the NoV-specific Ab to the poly (dop)-MNPs-Ag NCs by simply maintaining a reaction in Tris-HCl buffer at pH 8.5 (Chaiendoo et al., 2019).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Working principle and design of the dual-signaling biosensor (CSSFEI)
ce:section.ce:para: The design and operating principle of the proposed dual-modality detection of NoV are shown in Scheme 1c. The NoV-specific detection protocol was initiated by an immunoreaction of target NoV-LPs/NoV with Ab-conjugated-poly (dop)-MNPs-Ag NCs to form immunocomplexes (Scheme 1c, Step 1). Thereafter, Ab-conjugated-S-agCDs were added to generate CSSFEI constructs, then magnetically extracted, enriched, and redispersed in fresh buffer solutions (Scheme 1c, Step 2), followed by sequential detections using FL and SERS modalities (Scheme 1c, Steps 3 and 4). FL spectra were easily recorded on 96-well microplates using CSSFEI solutions. Next, the CSSFEI aliquots already measured by FL modality were transferred to SLG substrates for SERS modality detection. Thanks to the FL signal of S-agCD, it was possible to accurately monitor the CSSFEI “hotspots” areas on the SLG substrates, which enabled fast and efficient SERS detection of the target NoV. In order to effectively monitor the deposited CSSFEI constructs and specific “hotspots” on the substrates, FL confocal imaging was performed, which served as a guide to locating areas with the maximum concentration of CSSFEI for optimized SERS activity and sensitivity. The FL confocal imaging-assisted SERS detection strategy effectively demonstrates the combined advantages of the dual-modality approach used in designing our CSSFEI biosensor, thereby fast-tracking the SERS modality for the ultrasensitive detection of NoV. This approach has been used for precise bio-imaging and multiplexed detection of cancer biomarkers and biomolecules (Alvarez-Puebla et al., 2018; Chang et al., 2017; Niu et al., 2014; Zou et al., 2015). In addition, the use of chemically active SLG substrates for the SERS modality instead of bare glass substrates was envisioned to mitigate the likely FL interference of the SERS signals. However, for this particular study, FL interference can be excluded for two obvious reasons. The first is the FL quenching effect of graphene (SLG) due to their superior electrons transport system (Kang et al., 2015). Second, at an excitation of 532 nm used for SERS detection, the FL signal of S-agCD is weak. Therefore, the use of SLG synergizes the electromagnetic (EM) enhancement mechanism provided by plasmonic Ag NCs with the chemical mechanism of the SLG substrates (Kang et al., 2015; Ling et al., 2015), providing an overall SERS enhancement factor (EF) of ⁓108 compared to the EF of ⁓105 obtained for the bare glass substrate. This indicates that while bare glass substrates can still be used for the SERS modality detection, chemically active 2D SLG substrate can provide better sensitivity.

NoV-LPs were used for the proof-of-concept investigations of the dual-modality biosensor. Under the optimized bioassay protocols and control experiments (details in Figs. S7–S10 of the Supplementary data S9), the S-agCDs immuncomplexed to the poly (dop)-MNPs-Ag NCs increased proportionally with the target NoV-LPs/NoV concentrations, resulting in the dual-amplification of the FL and SERS signals of the S-agCDs. The FL spectra of the CSSFEI in PBS and 10% human serum are shown in Fig. 2 a and b, respectively. NoV-LPs in the concentration range of 1 fg mL−1 – 10 ng mL−1 were incubated with Ab-poly (dop)-MNPs-Ag NCs and Ab-S-agCDs to form the CSSFEI immunocomplex. The corresponding calibration plots, Fig. 2c, show linear dynamic correlations in the range of 10 fg mL−1 – 10 ng mL−1 of target NoV-LPs versus FL signals. Based on the calibration plots results, the limits of detection (LODs) were evaluated (details in Fig. S11 of the Supplementary data S10). LOD values for the FL modality were obtained at 5.8 and 6.5 fg mL−1 in PBS and 10% in human serum, respectively. The results confirmed that NoV bioassays could be safely performed in complex biological media. The detection in 10% human serum showed no significant difference in enhanced FL intensities compared to PBS medium. Thanks to the magnetic enrichment of the CSSFEI immunocomplex which facilitated a matrix-free detection of NoV-LPs in human serum and was extremely helpful in clinical NoV detection in the heavily matrix-loaded fecal specimen.

In a second step, the CSSFEI immunocomplexes containing NoV-LPs at concentrations ranging from 1 fg mL−1 – 10 ng mL−1 were transferred to prepared SLG-coated substrates for FL confocal imaging and SERS analysis. The advantage of the FL modality through confocal imaging was used to track the Ab-S-agCDs@Ab-poly (dop)-MNPs-Ag NCs (CSSFEI) immunocomplexes, followed by a dedicated tracking of identified “hotspots” regions prior to the Raman mapping procedures for an efficient and reliable SERS detection of target NoV-LPs/NoV. The confocal images of the CSSFEI at 1 pg mL−1 and 10 ng mL−1 are shown in Fig. 2d–f.

The FL signals of the S-agCDs can be clearly observed, which made it easy to track the “hotspots” regions of the CSSFEI on the SLG substrates. Consequently, the characteristic Raman band of S-agCDs at 1342 cm−1 was used for the SERS mapping of the same confocal imaged SLG substrates, which displayed intense “hotspots” regions corresponding to CSSFEI immunocomplexes and/or aggregates at different concentrations of NoV-LPs, Fig. 3 a–c.

The corresponding SERS spectra generated from the optimized “hotspots” regions in PBS and 10% human serum, respectively, are shown in Fig. 3d and e. The associated calibration (log-log transformation) plots displayed strong linearity across a wide dynamic range of 1 fg mL−1 – 10 ng mL−1 (Fig. 3f), with a broader detection range than the FL modality (Fig. 2c). The SERS modality recorded ultrasensitive LODs of 0.1 and 0.95 fg mL−1 in PBS and 10% human serum, respectively. The excellent LOD values demonstrate the distinct benefit of integrating the SERS technique in the dual-modality biosensor. Therefore, the two-pronged approach for NoV detection in this work is innovative and advantageous in the sense that the FL modality can be used as a first step screening for NoV infection. Then SERS modality is used for specific follow-up and highly sensitive detection, especially at concentration levels not detectable by the FL modality, to provide resolutely fast and reliable analytical quantification data in clinical settings.

Furthermore, the ultrasensitive level NoV-LPs detection that was achieved by utilizing our designed dual-modality system compared to some of the approaches listed in Table S1 clearly demonstrate the proposed bioassays' superior efficiency. This can be ascribed to the highly sensitive SERS modality detection, as well as the specifically deployed FL confocal guided SERS mapping, which offered some insights into the spatial distribution of “hotspots” regions with strong optical enhancement activities due to the LSPR effects of plasmonic Ag NCs. The observed LSPR-induced dual optical enhancements can be attributed to the implementation of local optical fields or “hotspots” initiated by the plasmon-induced electronic coupling activity between S-agCDs and poly (dop)-MNPs-Ag NCs, mediated by the target NoV. Therefore, the integration of FL and SERS signals into the nanosized S-agCDs as a single optical probe is a natural outcome of our innovative nanoparticles’ synthesis, which can be highly beneficial for multiplexed pathogens detection and bio-imaging. Importantly, rapid screening of NoV can be achieved using the FL modality and sensitive detection by the SERS modality, yielding more comprehensive and reliable analytical results.

Encouraged by the excellent sensitivity and quality analytical results achieved by the “hotspots” tracked SERS detection of NoV-LPs, clinical NoV subtype GII.3 specimens extracted from infected patients' feces were analyzed by the developed dual-modality bioassays. Clinical NoV of 101–106 RNA copies mL−1 was employed for this study at the optimized regimens. The FL modality results and the associated calibration plot in Fig. 4 a and b demonstrate that a LOD of ⁓ 80 RNA copies mL−1 could be attained for clinical NoV bioassays in less than 5 min. For the SERS modality detection, Fig. 4c and d, an impressive LOD of ⁓ 10 RNA copies mL−1 was obtained due to the dedicated tracking of the “hotspots” prior to SERS data collection, highlighting the SERS modality's broad relevance to the overall clinical NoV detection. This is equally owed to the magnetic enrichment protocol adopted and the integrated graphene-based SLG substrate for synergized SERS enhancement mechanisms (Kang et al., 2015; Lee et al., 2019).

Furthermore, the sensitivities and accuracy of the proposed biosensor were comparatively validated against commercially available ELISA and lateral flow NoV detection kits, Fig. S12 in the Supplementary data S11. Excitingly, only NoV RNA up to ~103 or 104 copies mL−1 could be detected by the test kits, compared to the less than ⁓10 RNA copies mL−1 detected by our dual-modality biosensor (Table S2). These results further reveal that the developed biosensor provides promising insights into significant advancements in the diagnosis of NoV in clinical samples using the CSSFEI biosensing system proposed in this work.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Specific and reproducible detection of NoV by CSSFEI dual-modality biosensor
ce:section.ce:para: A critical performance appraisal of robust biosensors is the quality and highly selective signal transduction in the presence of non-target analytes and/or the precise recognition of target analytes. Some selected viruses such as influenza virus A/H3N2, dengue virus-like particles serotype 2 (DENV-LPs), Zika virus, hepatitis E virus-like particles (HEV-LPs), and chikungunya virus cell lysate (ChikV) were examined separately to scrutinize the specificity of the novel bio-detection system for the target virus. The selectivity test results for the FL or SERS modality in Fig. 5 a and b show that the target NoV immunoassays can be carried out without interference up to 5 × 104 copies mL−1 or 5 μg mL−1 of the screened viruses. This demonstrates the characteristics of the developed CSSFEI-based biosensor with a dual readout capacity of being selective towards the target virus.

In addition, the SERS mode reproducibility and uniformity of the SLG substrates were demonstrated by recording the average intensity of the SERS signals at 1342 cm−1 from one hundred (100) random spots on the SLG substrate using the 1 ng mL−1 of NoV-LPs (Fig. 5c). The calculated RSD was pegged at 7.5%, indicating a deviation <10% (Fig. 5d), thus showing excellent spot-to-spot reproducibility of the CSSFEI on the SLG substrate, as well as the SERS-based modality for a reliable diagnostic application.

Finally, in comparison to conventional single-mode bioassays, our bimodal CSSFEI NoV bioassays exhibit the following benefits. The FL and SERS detection modalities are merged into a single biosensing system, leveraging wide linear range NoV detection with significantly enhanced sensitivity and accuracy. Also, our biosensor can generate two readouts for a specific NoV- antigen-antibody immunoreaction, potentially demonstrating the overall reliability of the biosensor and the detection results. Other advantages include rapid magnetic pretreatments that benefit the target virus separation from a strong sample matrix, allowing negligible background noise and strong anti-interference capability.

In this study, a high-performance dual-modality FL and SERS biosensor for NoV bioassay was developed. The rapid FL modality through confocal imaging was fully integrated for SERS “hotspots” tracking to efficiently utilize the ultrasensitive SERS modality, which would otherwise be time-consuming. The FL modality achieved an LOD of ⁓80 copies mL−1 for NoV detection in fecal specimens. The SERS-based detections were characterized with the highest sensitivities of 0.1 fg mL−1 for NoV-LPs and ⁓10 copies mL−1 for NoV. The FL modality's detection limit, albeit being faster, is a drawback of this approach because this biosensor was unable to achieve dual confirmation of the most critical low concentration of NoV in real specimens. As a result, combining FL and SERS for NoV pathogen detection provided an attractive opportunity to capitalize on the complementary benefits of both techniques, resulting in a thorough and reliable NoV detection while simultaneously overcoming the inherent limitations of both methods. In the future, the modification of the (nano) materials and/or system with a colorimetric modality might be a suitable option for expanding the biosensor's tremendous multiplexing capacity. Overall, the proposed dual-modality biosensor has a high quantitative potential combined with a broad linear detection range, making it excellent for preliminary testing (FL) and sensitive NoV viral pathogen detection (SERS modality) in both clinical settings and virus-infected environments requiring quick confirmation.

Ojodomo J. Achadu: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Writing – original draft. Fuyuki Abe: Resources. Farzana Hossain: Investigation, Formal analysis. Fahmida Nasrin: Electron microscopy, confocal imaging. Masahito Yamazaki: Resources. Tetsuro Suzuki: Resources. Enoch Y. Park: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: OJA gratefully thanks the Japan Society for the Promotion of Science (JSPS) for a Postdoctoral Fellowship for Research in Japan (Standard) (Grant No. 19F19348). The Japan Agency for Medical Research and Development partly supported this work (20hm0102080h0001).
